text: The surgeon administers a perianal nerve block to the patient, aiming to reduce postoperative pain and relax the sphincter, which will visually result in a wider intersphincteric plane.
The surgeon identifies the intersphincteric plane and inserts a probe into the fistula tract, preparing to open the plane.
The surgeon removes the seton, followed by the diathermic opening of the intersphincteric plane, revealing the underlying tissue structures.
The surgeon identifies the fistula tract and isolates it within the intersphincteric plane. The probe remains fixated within the tract, providing a clear visual guide for the isolation process.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the fistula tract, encircling it within the intersphincteric plane.
The surgeon removes the probe from the fistula tract and ligates the tract near the internal opening. The tract is then cut close to the internal opening.
The surgeon sutures the fistula tract at the site of the internal opening. The matured fistula tract is visible as a fibrotic tube.
The surgeon inspects the internal opening of the fistula tract to confirm closure following suturing, examining the area for any visible signs of remaining openings.
The surgeon inserts a probe to locate the fistula tract at the site of the external opening, followed by suturing this part of the tract.
The surgeon checks the closure of the fistula tract before closing the wound with sutures. The external opening is then excised and corrected.
The surgeon excises the subcutaneous fistula tract, creating a fresh wound that extends to the level of the external sphincter. The fibrotic tube of the fistula tract is visibly removed.
The surgeon inserts paraffin gauze into the surgical site, completing the procedure.
